data.title: Mixed Messages On U.S. Jobs Will Keep The Fed Hawkish
data.description:
By James Knightley, Chief International Economist, US

The September US jobs report (delayed from its 3 October scheduled release date) is firmer than the consensus predicted at +119,000 additions to non-farm payrolls versus 51,000 expected. We imagined the risks were always skewed to the upside, given the Federal Reserve had a good idea of its contents at the October FOMC when it cut rates by 25bp but adopted a more hawkish posture on future moves. This was likely one of the triggers.

Change in private payrolls (000s)

Nonetheless, the report isn't universally strong, with 33k downward revisions to the previous two months. The breakdown shows that it is the three usual suspects that contributed virtually all the jobs: leisure & hospitality +47k, government +22k and private education & healthcare services +59k. Remember, the federal government workers who accepted deals to leave won't drop off until the October report, which will now be combined with the November report and released on 16 December.

Meanwhile, the unemployment rate rose to 4.4% from 4.3%, and wage growth rose only 0.2% month-on-month. The unemployment rate comes from a separate survey of households. This showed a 470,000 increase in the labour force, but only 251,000 found jobs, so unemployment grew by 219,000. This survey tends to be more volatile than the establishment survey of firms that generates the non-farm payrolls number.

Consumer sentiment on jobs vs change in private payrolls

Given that we won't receive any more official jobs data ahead of the 10 December FOMC meeting, and we aren't confident on the inflation release schedule, it is understandable that the market is only pricing a 35% chance of a 25bp cut. That said, the probability is up from 24% at 8:00am, ahead of the release. It looks as though the market may be focusing a little more on the unemployment rate creeping higher. It is true to say that business surveys paint a gloomier picture of the jobs market, such as the ADP report and the ISM indicators, but we are going to need to see even more weakness to get the Fed to move in three weeks' time.

Charts, such as the one above, suggest the Fed will have more work to do in 2026. Workers remain very downbeat on the jobs market, and the relationship shows that over the past 50 years, workers see and feel changes before they show up in the official data. That doesn’t bode well for upcoming jobs reports.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Griffon: Decent Q4 And FY25 Results, But Tariff Impacts Still Linger
data.description:
Recap

Earlier this year, I covered Griffon Corporation (GFF) following their Q3 earnings call. At that time, the stock was trading for about $76 per share and trading for a small discount based on my FCF valuation model. I rated the stock a Hold as revenue had decreased significantly in Q3 for the Consumer and Professional Products segment as a result of ongoing tariff issues. Since my initial write-up, the stock has declined about 9%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) has risen by 3%.

Yesterday Griffon announced their Q4 and fiscal 2025 results. Let's take a look at the release and see if things have improved for the CPP segment, and examine the current valuation based on updated FCF numbers.

Q4 & Full-Year 2025 Earnings

Griffon announced their Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings before the market opened on Wednesday, November 19th. Overall, the earnings release was pretty positive as the quarterly numbers beat estimates on both ends. Non-GAAP EPS was $1.54 for the quarter topping the consensus by $0.03, on revenue of $662.2M which exceeded analyst expectations by almost $31M and was up minimally compared to the prior year. Fiscal year revenue shrank 4% to $2.5B from 2.6B, while an impairment charge hampered 2025 EPS numbers which came in at $1.09 per share, compared to $4.23 in FY24.

The Home and Building Products division saw quarterly revenue climb 3% YoY to more than $420M from $406.5M, as a result of product mix and favorable pricing adjustments. Additionally, demand for Commercial products was strong, which more than offset weaker demand from the Residential segment. Adjusted EBITDA was flat YoY as the increase in revenue was completely negated by a rise in material costs as well as increased wages.

For the fiscal year, revenue for the HBP segment slipped 2% overall, or $4.5M, with the Commercial division seeing a decline of $8M, or 1.1%, while Residential Repair and New Construction sales rose minimally. The adjusted EBITDA for the year shrank by 1% to $494.6M for same reasons the quarterly number was flat.

The Consumer and Professional Products segment struggled with quarterly sales declining $12M, or 4.4% year-over-year. Tariffs were the major driver here leading to an 8% drop in volume, partially offset by a beneficial price and product mix. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was down minimally as the company made gains in sourcing more U.S. products, despite the lower volumes.

Overall, FY25 revenue for CPP fell more than $100M, or 10%, as the tariffs effected this line of business significantly including weak demand in both the U.S. and U.K, leading to a YoY volume decrease of close to 12%. These declines were partially offset by a rise in organic sales in Australia, which also saw continued tailwinds from the 2024 Pope acquisition. Amazingly, adjusted EBITDA rose nearly 20% to $85.5M, despite the obvious struggles the CPP segment faced, as reduced administrative costs and U.S. sourcing more than offset foreign exchange headwinds.

Griffon also announced a robust 22.2% dividend increase, raising it from $0.18 to $0.22 per quarter and giving GFF a forward dividend yield 1.27%. In recent years, the company has grown its dividend at a rapid pace with its 3-year CAGR at more than 25%, while its 5 & 10-year dividend growth rates are strong too at 19.1% and 16.2%, respectively. I'd expect to see the dividend continue to increase sharply, as the company's payout ratio is below 20% leaving ample room for future dividend growth.

Management projects 2026 revenue to be flat, while adjusted EBITDA is anticipated to rise more than 10%, which follows just a 2% increase from 2024 to 2025.

The market was encouraged by the quarterly and fiscal results with shares of Griffon rising close to 7% in early trading, before ending the day up more than 3.5%.

The full earnings call can be read/listened to here.

Risks/Headwinds

Griffon delivered resilient results despite a challenging environment. The company will need to maintain discipline to sustain these promising results.

The obvious headwind facing Griffon are the tariffs, which effected the company's 2025 results. Management has made strides to reduce the effects from them, particularly in the CPP segment; however, any additional tariffs or trade disputes could exacerbate this problem.

Griffon relies on growth by acquisition as one of the main ways to expand, as many company do. Having said that, in Q3 2025 the company incurred a significant impairment charge of more than $200M related to the Hunter Fan deal. Although the company was able to absorb that impact, it does give me concern as an investor that management could overpay for future acquisitions leading to additional financial impacts.

One final risk facing Griffon, is the reliance on the housing market, which the Home and Building Products segment is strongly linked to. Any material downtown in the housing market could lead to reduced demand for HBP products and significantly effect earnings.

Valuation

The last piece of this puzzle is to determine if Griffon is trading for an attractive valuation, and as usual we'll look at it from a free cash flow perspective.

I estimate a stock's implied fair value based on its free cash flow yield and specifically how the current yield compares to the company's historical average. This helps me gauge how much investors have usually been willing to pay for a company's free cash flow and how attractive the stock looks relative to that history. The analysis is calculated on 12 years of daily historical price data capturing a rolling 5-year average FCF yield for the past 7 years. The valuation chart below compares Griffon's actual share price and the fair value range calculated using this methodology.

The main assumption behind this valuation method is that investors have paid a fair price for the stock over the past 5 years on average, but since no valuation method is perfect, there is a +/-10% margin of error applied to the fair value range.

My custom free cash flow valuation model indicates Griffon is trading for a moderate discount to its fair value, with a current price just below $70 and a fair price closer to $80. Estimating the fair value of Griffin can be difficult due to drastic shifts in the growth of their FCF. For example, the 5-year FCF CAGR is just below 30%, while the 3-year CAGR is above 140%, so this volatility is something investors should take into account.

The long-term expected rate of return for Griffon was previously 13.6%, and is up marginally since my initial coverage to 14.6%. The components of this estimate are a forward dividend yield of 1.27%, a return to fair value factor of 2.47%, and an expected earnings growth rate of 10.87%.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, the stock has struggled since my initial coverage, declining around 9%. The HBP segment continues to see decent results, while the CPP segment has been faced with glaring impacts from tariffs. The company raised its dividend by 22%, indicating management's confidence to in their current and future results. Griffon's valuation is more appealing than it was previously, and I think investors should consider this stock following these results and a healthy dividend bump.

Ultimately, I am going to maintain a Hold rating, as the company is still dealing with lingering effects from the tariffs. It'll take some time before those can be mitigated in a considerable way.

data.title: Nuclear Projects Don't Have To Be Late And Overbudget
data.description:
By Stacey Morris, CFA

One of the pushbacks to nuclear power is its high, upfront capital costs, which can be potentially exacerbated by cost overruns and delays. This was certainly the case for the last large reactors built in the U.S. in Georgia (Vogtle 3 and 4), and the reactors currently under construction in the UK have also seen challenges.

While the nuclear projects likely to end up in the newsfeeds of U.S. investors have faced setbacks, other countries have seen better execution on large reactor projects. Admittedly, countries like China benefit from standardization and repetition with multiple projects under construction. However, the United Arab Emirates (UAE) provides an example of a new entrant to nuclear power that saw very strong execution on building reactors in recent years.

The UAE Example

Historically reliant on fossil fuels, the UAE turned to nuclear energy to secure emission-free power to fuel economic growth. Its four-reactor Barakah plant proves that nuclear power projects can be delivered on schedule and on budget. The first unit took eight years from the initial concrete pour to the start of operations, and efficiencies were realized with the subsequent units. For context, Vogtle 3 in the U.S. took 14 years to complete and was roughly seven years behind schedule.

Rigorous planning, global collaboration, and thorough budgeting are some of the key elements of the UAE’s approach that contributed to its overall success. Potential project partners were evaluated for a year by a team of 75 nuclear experts from around the world.

Ultimately, Korea Electric Power Corporation (KEPCO) was chosen to design, build, and support the operations of the Barakah Plant. Nuclear reactors that came on-line in 2016 and 2019 in South Korea using the same technology (APR 1400) served as reference plants for Barakah. KEPCO is a constituent of the Range Nuclear Renaissance Index (NUKZX).

Why do some projects struggle?

Nuclear projects can be challenging, as they’re often first-of-a-kind facilities, deploying a design that has not been used before. This can understandably create difficulties. In practicality, the third or fourth version is easier than the first, and later iterations benefit from prior learnings. Regulations can also contribute to delays and cost overruns, with nuclear projects subject to significant oversight.

Nuclear development becomes better and more efficient with repetition. Certainly, there are efficiency gains from the first to the second or fourth unit on the same site. More broadly, multiple projects under construction in a country would drive more robust supply chains and a stronger workforce. As the U.S. seeks to triple nuclear power capacity by 2050, these are key areas that need support and have been an emphasis in executive orders from the Trump administration (read more).

Nuclear isn’t simple, but has clear advantages

Admittedly, nuclear power plants are costly and time-intensive to build, but they also generate zero-emission, reliable baseload power for decades. As of April 2025, the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission had approved 12 reactors to operate for 80 years, and was reviewing applications from an additional five reactors to extend their licenses to 80 years. Upfront costs are more manageable when viewed through the lens of 80 or more years of operation. For context, the life span for less-reliable solar panels or wind turbines may be 20-30 years.

Nuclear is also not unique in taking years to develop. Consider oil and gas, where production depletes over time compared to nuclear’s steady output. According to the International Energy Agency, it typically takes 20 years between when exploration licenses are granted and hydrocarbons are actually produced.

Bottom Line

Nuclear power projects are expensive and take time. However, their longevity of operation, reliability, and emission-free power generation represent distinct advantages. The UAE provides a prime example of solid execution on large reactors, proving nuclear projects do not have to be plagued with delays and cost overruns.

NUKZX is the underlying index for the Range Nuclear Renaissance Index ETF (NUKZ).

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2025. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com.

Original Post

data.title: Nuvation Bio: Taking Care With Rapid Rises In Valuation (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Topline Summary And Update

Nuvation Bio Inc. (NUVB) is a company that I and other analysts on Seeking Alpha have maintained optimism for, given its entry into the management of biomarker-driven non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). However, in recent days at the time of writing, the company has vaulted in market value, rising to over $1.5 billion compared with around $800 million the last time I reiterated a Buy sentiment on the stock, despite some reservations about market penetration for their newly approved agent. Today, I want to summarize what we know about recent developments for NUVB stock, and outline the case for not jumping in too hastily based on these recent moves.

Pipeline Updates

Taletrectinib

NUVB received approval for the ROS1 inhibitor taletrectinib to treat patients with advanced or metastatic NSCLC harboring a ROS1 alteration, with or without prior exposure to a ROS1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI). It joins the likes of crizotinib, entrectinib, and repotrectinib as an option for this target, which is found in approximately % to 2% of all new cases. This is a small proportion, for sure, but considering there are around 195,000 new cases of NSCLC per year (87% of the total number of lung cancer cases), this means that between 2000 and 4000 new patients with ROS1-positive NSCLC are being diagnosed.

As I've covered in my most recent article, there are signs that taletrectinib is a more active agent than crizotinib, based on an analysis of the TRUST-I and -II trials. But there is no direct comparison between taletrectinib and other options at this time. It is an active area of debate in the oncology community, which TKI would be best to lead with. Repotrectinib demonstrated a median PFS of 35.7 months, compared with 45.6 months for taletrectinib. These are not direct comparisons, but they provide a nominal signal that taletrectinib should be considered in the mix for first-line therapy.

More importantly, repotrectinib is associated with a 96% rate of treatment-related adverse events, most notably those related to off-target inhibition of Trk. Dizziness, in particular, is a common side effect associated with repotrectinib, occurring in 58% of patients. This led to dose interruptions, but no discontinuations of repotrectinib. In contrast, only 21% of patients experienced dizziness with taletrectinib, and toxicity was mostly related to low-grade elevation of liver enzymes.

Another point of conversation is which TKI to give after progression on prior therapy. Both repotrectinib and taletrectinib are effective against solvent front mutations that drive resistance to crizotinib (i.e., ROS1 G2032R mutation), whereas lorlatinib, which is an investigational next-generation ROS1 inhibitor, has a 0% response rate in this setting. But the more interesting development in this space is the emergence of third-generation TKIs, most notably Nuvalent's (NUVL) zidesamtinib, which I have covered in more detail recently. Zidesamtinib appears to be active in patients with prior exposure to repotrectinib and taletrectinib.

This raises the happy question of optimal sequencing, now that we have a wide range of different TKIs available to attack ROS1-positive NSCLC. The emergent "optimal" algorithm is unclear, with many oncologists likely to use crizotinib first, wait for resistance to develop, and then move on to a second-generation TKI like taletrectinib or repotrectinib. Getting the fog to lift on this optimal treatment sequence is going to be the existential puzzle for NUVB's near-term profitability.

NUVB management guides that they feel taletrectinib is emerging as the standard of care in this setting. Notably, the lack of crizotinib activity in the central nervous system has been its key challenge in the space of ROS1-positive NSCLC, and it makes this move toward using second-generation TKIs in the frontline setting more plausible.

NUVB recently launched the phase 3 TRUST-IV trial to evaluate taletrectinib in patients with ROS1-positive early-stage NSCLC, with the hope of joining the likes of osimertinib and alectinib as adjuvant therapy options for patients with this actionable mutation. Taletrectinib has also been approved in the metastatic setting in China and Japan.

Safusidenib

NUVB is also continuing to work on an inhibitor of IDH1, with particular relevance in the management of certain forms of brain cancer called glioma. We've seen a lot of progress in the management of IDH1-mutated glioma in recent years, most notably with the approval of vorasidenib, and NUVB is positioning safusidenib as another option in this line.

NUVB discussed in their most recent earnings call that they estimate that sales of vorasidenib have reached over $550 million since launch, with an estimated $223 million in net revenue for Q2 2025 based on assessment of an investor update from US Royalty Pharma. This underscores what the company feels is a major commercial opportunity for a novel agent in this setting.

Rather than conduct a head-to-head study against vorasidenib, NUVB has begun a trial evaluating the use of safusidenib as maintenance therapy in high-grade glioma and high-risk, low-grade tumors. The trial will enroll approximately 300 patients, and it provides NUVB an opportunity to place safusidenib earlier in the standard of care than vorasidenib. The primary endpoint is PFS compared with placebo maintenance.

Financial Overview

At the end of Q3 2025, NUVB held $98.9 million in cash and equivalents, as well as $450.1 million in marketable securities. They had a total of $52.7 million in short- and long-term borrowings. Total revenues reached $13.1 million ($7.7 million from product revenues), set against $3.3 million for cost of revenue. Operating expenses reached $66.2 million.

After interest, the net loss for the quarter was $55.8 million. For the first 9 months of 2025, NUVB used $143.7 million to fund operations. At this burn rate, the company has a cash runway of between 11 and 12 quarters. NUVB management continues to guide that this cash runway should be sufficient to get the company to profitability.

Strengths And Risks

NUVB has stacked a good number of chips in their favor with the initial marketing of taletrectinib, particularly when you tie that to their long cash runway.

Strength: Encouraging Initial Launch Of Taletrectinib

We're only getting the most preliminary look at how taletrectinib will perform in the ROS1 market, but we've seen rapid growth of new patients undergoing treatment, and as discussed above, there is a case to be made for taletrectinib at pretty much any line of therapy (favorable tolerability and CNS penetrants in the first-line setting, activity after progression on other TKIs, including repotrectinib). Taken together, this suggests that most patients who are eligible to receive taletrectinib eventually will, even as next-generation TKIs enter the market.

It is premature to speculate on how quickly revenues will grow from this point, but it will be important to watch the quarter-over-quarter acceleration.

Strength: Pipeline Developing Beyond A Single Targeted Therapy

On top of that, safusidenib is showing signals that it could be a key player in the glioma market, potentially allowing it to tap significantly into what NUVB management believes is currently a market rapidly approaching $1 billion in annual sales (considering the estimated >$200 million in sales in Q2 2025 for vorasidenib). This would provide substantial diversification for their commercial pipeline, if successful.

Risk: Competition On All Fronts

Both ROS1 and IDH1 are competitive landscapes serving relatively small populations of patients. Even though there appear to be distinguishing features for both taletrectinib and safusidenib, novel therapies are coming all the time. In particular, it would be a mistake to ignore NUVL's zidesamtinib, which could end up being more well tolerated and active, posing a serious risk to second-generation TKIs. The emergence of novel therapies for these targets could end up being a major speed bump, and it's very difficult to predict whether NUVB's agents will continue to play the same role indefinitely.

Bottom-Line Summary

NUVB has recently undergone a major re-evaluation by the market, having more than doubled since my last coverage of the company back in June. Just in the past few days, the stock price has soared around 50%, due in part to the emerging narrative that taletrectinib sales could reach into the billions, although management has not provided specific projections just yet.

It also doesn't hurt that there is currently an unsubstantiated rumor floating around social media that NUVB is close to inking some kind of major partnership, although the nature of this is unknown (covered in part on X by Seeking Alpha's own Jonathan Faison). But of course, there are no credible sources on this particular point. I only mention it because of the timing of a rapid ascent in price coinciding with the emergence of this rumor on November 19.

All of this is great news for NUVB, but this rapid rise also signals to me that the market could be getting ahead of itself, as positive sentiments continue to flow in without yet having reached the sales execution needed to justify it. Remember that repotrectinib achieved only around $38 million in sales in 2024, and this has been a major sticking point for the bull case even before this recent run in stock price. This hype is buoyed further by revisions to price targets like B Riley Securities' $12 per share, and although I like the emergence of a positive narrative supporting my prior Buy sentiment, there is also a lot of near-term volatility put into play.

Long term, NUVB has a lot of opportunities, and for the patient among you (who are willing to take the risk that the company's novel trials in earlier-stage disease will pay off), NUVB continues to represent something that is worth buying and holding. However, between now and the next time I cover NUVB, I expect that a more favorable entry point is likely, and I am revising my sentiment to Hold, given the excessive risk of near-term volatility.

NUVB is absolutely worth watching, but I would be very reticent to jump in at these price levels before we know more about the sales trajectory of taletrectinib.

data.title: The Alpha Analyst: STCE ETF: AI-Driven Strategy For Crypto And Fintech Exposure
data.description:
Follow The Alpha Analyst on Seeking Alpha!

Follow STCE on Seeking Alpha!

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content is offered for information purposes only. Unless stated otherwise, any and all individuals participating in the video are third parties that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body. Unless stated otherwise, the views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole.

The accuracy and completeness of content shared cannot be guaranteed. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US investment adviser, or investment bank.

Daniel Snyder: Hey, everyone. Welcome back to another episode of ETF Spotlight. I'm Daniel Snyder. Today, we're jumping into the world of crypto and specifically an ETF by Charles Schwab that our analyst joining us today says is compelling, but also comes with risks. And of course, looking at the recent crypto scenario in the markets, the pullback is on. So, maybe this ETF reflects that as well, but maybe it produces the alpha going forward.

So today, we are joined by The Alpha Analyst himself who's been writing here on Seeking Alpha for the last year or so. So, welcome to the program. First things I would love to do is, just dive in. What is your background that you have in the space and financial markets and what really interested you in the crypto arena? Let's go ahead and start there.

Alpha Analyst: Yeah. So, I have around 15 to 20 years of experience in tech, technology. I was in software, and then I jumped into Quant trading, Quant model building, moved into risk for quite a decade, that was in Barclays. And, yeah, that's pretty much been my background. I have a risk-hat always on. And, unfortunately, STCE has not lived up to that risk-hat, but, again, when we dive into the details of STCE, we'll probably – I'll talk about how to play crypto. We can't be defensive in this space. So, but anyway, coming back to your question, that's about myself.

DS: Yeah. So, let's jump into it. So, you teased it already. STCE, which is the Schwab Crypto Thematic ETF. Can you give us a little bit of the inner workings? How is this ETF put together? And, I mean, I was looking at some of the holdings. Maybe you can touch on this as well. A lot of miners in there, it seems right now, but what is the basics of this ETF?

AA: Yeah. So, I think why I picked up STCE is, so there are several alternatives to STCE. If you want to bet on the crypto theme, the crypto ecosystem, and there are other ETFs also in play. There is BKCH, there is VanEck’s DAPP and there is Block. And you will generally see they do pretty close to each other. They are fairly correlated, but what sets STCE apart, what took my interest was the way they approach the methodology, which is a very well balanced – beautifully balanced middle ground of active and passive trading or rather active and passive methodologies if you want to put it that way.

They have taken out cognitive biases from the methodology. They've kept it active in a way, it is agile. So, the last time I saw STCE, when I was writing about it in September, it had a bunch of names that have now come out of the Top 10. It's that agile, which is something you will not see in passive plays, even in the crypto space.

So, STCE makes it very active, agile. And between the quarterly rebalances, it will allow for the momentum names to grow within the portfolio because it is fairly concentrated to most crypto ETFs are, but yeah, again, the differentiator, I think, though that stands the test of time. Performance shows in the last one year that it has been in the top one or two crypto ETFs consistently.

It may not be the top all the time. It is very high growth, high risk as well as we have seen in the past few weeks of crypto-led corrections. So, yeah, that's something that excited me. The way they approach it is through machine learning algorithm, which is at the backbone of this ETF. And that, as I mentioned, takes out the cognitive biases that even human active portfolio rebalancing does not in a lot of cases. And I have been there in trading desks. So, yeah, there are biases of doubling down on losing positions, etcetera. This takes that out of the equation.

It simply looks at publicly available datasets, publicly available documents, picks its stocks basis of a thematic beta, which is a score data science. It assigns some kind of a weight to the past holdings so that it's not completely a very radical maverick change from one rebalance to the other, but yet it allows changes.

I think if you look at the Top 10 holdings today, names like Bitfarm and HIVE are there in the Top 5 commanding somewhere around 12%, 13% of weight for the portfolio. This wasn't there in the Top 10 in September. So, that's the agility that is brought to the table, which is very important for crypto particularly. Crypto is a space where chasing momentum is the way to go. That's my take. And, over time you'll see the ups and downs, of course, like you have seen in STCE. It can be used tactically as well.

STCE, I think bounced 100% almost from the time I wrote. And that has been again, that's, sorry for digressing, but that has been one other key feature of STCE. Because of its agnostic methodology, which is more machine driven or machine learning driven, it has been able to pick out those non-traditional crypto plays. I saw NVIDIA in there. I saw PayPal in there. I saw Robinhood, another fintech and digital infrastructure names as well, which means you are not entirely reliant on the crypto rally per se or the crypto prices.

It's another matter that the crypto prices are indirectly linked to crypto adoption, but this can grow with crypto adoption if not crypto prices. Since the time I wrote, crypto prices haven't really made large moves, which I was expecting. This was heavily correlated to crypto prices right before I wrote. But it has been largely an offshoot of, you may call it accidental holdings in IREN and Cipher, which gained because of the AI pivot that they were into, rather than crypto-led boom.

So, that is the possibility that opens up with STCE. It will give you diversification across the crypto world, keep the thematical orientation quite pure as much as it can, but it will assign weights to certain names that can gain from other bullish factors like the AI data center push for IREN and Cipher.

So, yeah, that's pretty much the primary reason I think why I would overweight STCE in my portfolio. I want something that's reliably passive, but at the same time active and agile. So, it brings the best of both the worlds together. It is agile. It reacts very fast, which is very much required in the crypto space, but it doesn't do a trade everyday like, something like Block does, for example.

So, it waits for a quarterly rebalance, but when it does the rebalance it acknowledges it. It doesn't look at backward looking metrics as too much like fundamental factors or market caps and things. So, market caps react really late. It's already a backward looking metric. So, STCE brings in that agility, brings in that forward looking or rather momentum chasing angle to it, which is the way I think crypto should be played. Defensive doesn't work in crypto. And, yeah.

DS: If I can interrupt you for a second, I want to ask you this, because we're talking about the turnover of the names. So, we're talking about the turnover of the weights of the names within the portfolio. And the turnover I'm looking here on the Seeking Alpha symbol page, turnover 60%, and you're, of course, paying a 30 basis point expense ratio. And the space is always evolving so quickly. So, you have this diversified approach as you're saying with miners, and I know Coinbase is on here as well. So, you're talking about exchanges. You mentioned Robinhood. You mentioned a couple other names.

AA: Yes. Yeah.

DS: But why just diversify over the entire space in an ETF like this instead of just own the ETFs, which are like the pure Bitcoin plays in the space? What's the advantages and risk here?

AA: So, if you're asking me why, why not a pure Bitcoin related, co-related portfolio, is that your question, if I got it right?

DS: Yeah. Yeah. Exactly. So, diversifying, because with these names, you'll also get exposure to Ethereum and all the other coins that are trading on exchanges.

AA: Yeah. Yeah. Understood.

DS: Also the argument of people wanting only a sole source of maybe Bitcoin thinking that it’s the leader.

AA: Yeah. So, I think that is very well explained in the last rally. The 100% rally, if you look at it from a Bitcoin price perspective, it wasn't – Bitcoin didn't go anywhere. Almost didn't go anywhere throughout that rally, and the diversification helped because it can pick up other factors that mitigate your expectations of a Bitcoin price increase, if you will. And the other factor is that if, for example, all we need for more crypto adoption is crypto stability. It need not rise all the time. Crypto prices need not rise. But if it is stable and people adopt crypto, they use it in wallets. So, names like that will come into a portfolio like STCE, and that allows a company which is growing just because of adoption and not because of crypto price boom to rally. And that helps the cause of a very well diversified Crypto ETF.

I think most ETFs do that. It's not like the other ETFs like, BKCH, DAPP and the other ones I named, Block, they too have diversification. But this is proven by performance. In the last one year, it's been in the Top 2 as I mentioned, and it is agnostic. It is almost like, takes a call on the basis of data points rather than fundamentals or active selections.

DS: Now, earlier in the conversation I want to wrap up with this. Earlier in the conversation, you mentioned that crypto is a space that is heavily influenced by momentum, right? And I think that was the words you mentioned. Now, year-to-date, STCE as an ETF is up 49.5%, which is tremendous, right? We've had an amazing crypto rally this year, but in the last month alone, it's down, call it 30.3%. So, obviously, some would look at that and go, well, momentum is slowing, momentum is stopping, should I be worried here? Should we be worried about the momentum slowing down in the crypto space?

AA: I don't think so. People who are trading in crypto and investing in crypto should be ready for this, right. For example, if you look at the, again, as you mentioned, year-to-date, it's still high. So, it will be higher-highs and higher-lows. That's how the journey is going to be all over the world. In the U.S., there have been regulations to make crypto adoption a little more friendlier and transparent. This is happening across the board. It's happening in India. It's happening in Europe as well.

So, regulations are coming in. I think crypto adoption will go up. Crypto volatility should come down, that is when it becomes more adoption friendly, but, yes, I think volatility will be part of the game. Momentum plays will go through phases of dull share prices as well. I think dull is the wrong word here. Exciting share price moves, but, yes, there will be downsides. There will definitely be downsides, but there's nothing to be worried about per se right now. I had written another article about the markets being in a zone where it's not a unilateral upside move anymore, that holds true for crypto as well.

So, we should be looking out for a dumping down of the volatility levels. If that is happening, then that should be fine for a crypto investor in my view.

DS: Alright. That's a good place to end it right there. Let's go ahead and say thank you so much to The Alpha Analyst, everyone. If you want to go reach out and follow him on Seeking Alpha, highly encourage you to do that. Go follow some more of the research and keep an eye on STCE. If you're interested in the crypto space, as I know many of you are. Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with us today. Everyone, Alpha Analyst and I'm Daniel Snyder and we'll see you again here next week on the next ETF Spotlight.

Follow The Alpha Analyst on Seeking Alpha!

Follow STCE on Seeking Alpha!

data.title: Strathcona Resources: A Special Dividend Is Around The Corner
data.description:
Introduction

Strathcona Resources (SCR:CA) (OTCPK:STHRF) is a Canadian heavy oil producer with a current production rate in excess of 100,000 barrels of oil per day. The company recently "lost" out on its bid to acquire MEG Energy, but its shareholders will be rewarded with a C$10 special dividend per share.

A robust performance in Q3 despite a lower oil price

In the third quarter of the current financial year, the company produced approximately 116,200 barrels of oil-equivalent per day, with 99.6% of the total oil-equivalent production actually consisting of oil. There was a very small contribution from discontinued operations as Strathcona completed the sale of some of its assets in the first few days of the quarter.

The oil price was, of course, weaker than in the same quarter of last year, and Strathcona reported an average realized oil price of just under C$75 per barrel, which is almost 10% lower than the average realized price in the third quarter of 2024. This resulted in a total revenue of just over C$1B before taking royalties into consideration, and a net revenue of approximately C$903M, and this includes a C$27M loss on existing hedges.

As you can see in the income statement above, the company definitely remained profitable as it recorded a pre-tax profit of C$201M and a net profit of almost C$145M, which works out to C$0.68 per share. This also brought the 9M 2025 EPS to C$2.13 per share despite recording a C$85M hedge loss, partially compensated by a C$24M FX gain.

I always like to have a look at the cash flow statement as well, as that determines how much the company can spend on its investments. As you can see below, the reported operating cash flow was approximately C$289M, but this includes almost C$67M in working capital changes but excludes roughly C$10.5M in lease payments. This means the adjusted operating cash flow was approximately C$335M in the quarter.

The total capex was C$282M, which means Strathcona reported a net free cash flow of approximately C$52M, or around C$0.25 per share, for the quarter. While that sounds somewhat disappointing, keep in mind a substantial portion of the capex represents growth capex. Subsequent to the recent asset sales, the annual sustaining capex should be around C$550M (as shown below), which represents approximately C$140M per quarter.

Applying that number to the Q3 results, the underlying net free cash flow generated by Strathcona is approximately C$195M, representing C$0.90 per share.

Strathcona "lost" out on MEG Energy – or is it a winner?

Strathcona Resources really increased awareness about the company when it entered into the race to acquire MEG Energy after the latter entered into an agreement with Cenovus Energy (CVE) (CVE:CA) to be acquired. Strathcona acquired a stake on the open market and continued to buy stock in an attempt to block the deal by vocally opposing it, and this ultimately resulted in Cenovus having to increase its offer to get the deal sealed.

Some market commentators looked at the end result as "Strathcona failed to land MEG Energy," but I’m not so sure Strathcona is the loser in this story.

First of all, it's making money on its position in MEG Energy, and this, in combination with recent asset sales, will allow the company to pay a special dividend of C$10 per share. This means the company is keeping its word, as it has always said it would pay that special dividend if its bid for MEG Energy were to be unsuccessful.

Secondly, as part of the agreement to cease its resistance against the Cenovus offer, it entered into an asset purchase agreement with the latter, allowing it to purchase about 5,000 boe/day of production for just C$150M. This is a cheap deal, and it also sparked the interest of the regulator, which required MEG Energy to provide additional details on this side deal before the vote was allowed to happen.

In any case, Strathcona is now very cashed up after the asset sale, and an upcoming shareholders meeting will decide on a C$10/share special dividend. This will cost the company approximately C$2.14B and will be funded from existing cash on hand while drawing down more funds from its line of credit (which was repaid subsequent to the asset sales).

For now, the company’s guidance for 2026 remains unchanged as it expects to produce 115,000-125,000 boe/day while maintaining a C$1B capex spending plan. However, it will provide an update once its acquisition of the Cenovus assets closes in the next few weeks.

Investment Thesis

Strathcona still represents a good proxy to gain exposure to the oil price, and this could still warrant a "buy" rating, although investors are warned there may be some dividend-chasing money in the stock now. Given the tax regulations in my jurisdiction, I have sold my position in Strathcona, as capital gains enjoy a better tax regime than (special) dividends.

That being said, I will be looking to get back in the stock after its ex-dividend date (depending on where it is trading, of course), as my decision to sell is solely based on the fiscal implications.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Steven Madden: The Shoe Is On The Wrong Foot (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in February of this year, I decided to take a fresh look at Steven Madden (SHOO). From the time I had originally rated the company a ‘buy’ in September of 2022 until I subsequently downgraded it back in July of 2024, the stock outperformed the market, rising 52.5% compared to the 45% increase that the S&P 500 has achieved. But after such a monumental on up the hill, so to speak, I made the decision to downgrade it. Since my most recent article reaffirming it as a ‘hold’ candidate, however, the stock has underperformed the market, rising only 4.8% compared to the 13% rise that the S&P 500 has seen.

To be honest with you, I'm not surprised by this. Recent revenue figures provided by management have been promising. After a strong 2024 fiscal year, revenue continued decline through the first nine months of 2025. But profitability and cash flows have become problematic. From 2023 to 2024, these figures were mixed. But this year, profitability and cash flows are down across the board. I would be more optimistic if shares of the business were cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies. By considering how the stock is priced, and factoring in the issues that it's facing right now, I think that downgrading it to a soft ‘sell’ is the right choice.

Talk about pain

I don't think it would be terribly useful to dig too much into the financial data from 2023 and 2024. But for context, in the chart above, you can see what the company reported for those windows of time. Revenue increased nicely, but profitability declined. We also saw a contraction in operating cash flow. But on the other hand, adjusted net income, adjusted operating cash flow, and EBITDA all improved. If we were just seeing more of the same for this year, I would probably consider upgrading it. Because using last year's figures, shares really are not that expensive. But unfortunately, the picture looks different now.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025. Revenue for the company, a firm that focuses on marketing and selling fashion forward branded and private label footwear, accessories, and apparel, still managed to grow, climbing from $1.70 billion to $1.78 billion. This jump in sales was driven, according to management, by a strong showing when it came to its Direct-To-Consumer segment. Overall revenue skyrocketed from $374.2 million to $529.1 million. Management attributed this increase of 29.7% from an extra 41.4% surge coming from incremental revenue from brick-and-mortar stores, concessions, and digital businesses that the company picked up as a result of its acquisition of Kurt Geiger. This London-based footwear and accessories brand was purchased by the company in a deal valued at $390.5 million at closing, as well as $12.9 million of contingent consideration. Without this acquisition, revenue for this segment would have fallen. But that's not the only area where organic weakness was.

For instance, the Wholesale Footwear segment for the business reported a decline in revenue from $832 million to $782.8 million. Meanwhile, the Wholesale Accessories/Apparel segment reported a drop from $487.3 million to $459.8 million. Management blamed this top line pane on the impact of tariffs. Specifically, they said that their off price and mass merchant customers ended up not buying as much because of the higher costs that tariffs mean for the business.

Even though revenue for the company improved nicely, profitability took a heck of a hit. Net income plummeted from $134.6 million to $21.5 million. Some of this was because of transaction related costs, including $38.8 million of expenses on the compensation side associated with acquisition related sellers proceeds. But even on an adjusted basis, net income for the business dropped from $153 million to $86.6 million. Once again, tariffs, as well as the addition of the lower gross margin concessions associated with the purchase of Kurt Geiger, negatively affected the business. Operating cash flow ended up falling from $94.2 million to $67.6 million. Even if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a substantial drop from $191.6 million down to $70.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company declined from $219.2 million to $68.4 million.

Management expects the aforementioned acquisition to be instrumental in pushing revenue up year over year in the final quarter of this year by between 27% and 30%. But in addition to this, the company stands to benefit from a normalization on the wholesale side even as management implements strategic pricing actions and sourcing initiatives aimed at improving its own gross profit margin. Some of the benefit the company sees should come from marketing efforts with Millennials and Gen Z aimed at fueling demand for its Steve Madden brand, especially when it comes to boots, dress shoes, loafers, and more. Expected earnings per share for the final quarter should be between $0.41 and $0.46, which would take adjusted net profits to $31 million at the midpoint. It is worth noting, however, that even with this adjusted net income would be below the $39.4 million the company reported the same time last year.

If we take the total estimate for adjusted net income this year $117.6 million, which is down drastically from the $192.4 million the company reported for last year, and if we take results that we have already seen so far this year, we would expect adjusted operating cash flow to be about $142.7 million and for EBITDA to come in at around $164 million. Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These multiples are rather lofty, though not necessarily in the overvalued range. Compared to other similar firms, however, the stock is certainly pricey. In the table below, I stacked it up against five such enterprises. Four of the five ended up being cheaper than it is on both a price to earnings basis and on a price to operating cash flow basis. Meanwhile, on an EV to EBITDA basis, our prospect ended up being the most expensive of the group.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Steven Madden 23.1 19.0 17.9
Wolverine World Wide (WWW) 16.2 8.9 15.8
Rocky Brands (RCKY) 10.7 7.2 7.9
Crocs (CROX) 26.2 5.8 5.5
Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK) 12.1 11.5 8.1
Birkenstock Holdings (BIRK) 21.8 20.3 12.2

Takeaway

Normally, I wouldn't take such a drastic action as downgrading Steven Madden in response to these numbers alone. However, I am also worried about the economy more broadly. I believe that the US is heading toward a recession, and between the decrease in consumer demand and the painful impact of tariffs that remain a big uncertainty, I would argue that downgrading the company to a soft ‘sell’ is the right move.

data.title: Nvidia: Cockroaches Will Survive A Bubble Pop
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) dropped its Q3 '26 results on an angsty market and got a nod of approval with the stock up about 5% in pre-market on the outlook for the January quarter, outpacing consensus expectations and whisper numbers for $65B versus $61.8B and $64B, respectively, plus a guide for gross margins to revisit the 75% level. Tech stocks are trading up on the results, and it looks like markets are in for the first breather this month.

Heading into this print, our eyes were on Blackwell, specifically the ramp of GB300. We are sniffing around the right place, with GB300 accounting for two-thirds of Blackwell revenue this quarter. For the next two quarters, it’s all about Blackwell with Hopper (including H20) making up an “insignificant” part of Q3 revenue; CEO Jensen Huang notes in the release that “Blackwell sales are off the charts, and cloud GPUs are sold out...”

Data center sales for the quarter grew 25% to $51.2B (ahead of expected $49.3B), with compute growing 27% sequentially to $43B while networking grew 13% to $8.2B. The bonus of the Blackwell Ultra ramp is that it boosts networking revenue; it is designed for massive-scale use using NVL (which means dual-GPU products that are connected via high-speed NVLink interconnect) and Ethernet and Infiniband solutions.

The results this quarter were good, but they looked great against looming fears of an AI bubble and circular financing. Q3 revenue grew 22% Q/Q to $57B, beating expectations for $55B while non-GAAP gross margins expanded 90 basis points to 73.6%. The stock has been trading down heading into print pretty much flat on the three-month chart, shown below, and roughly flat QTD. Panic was augmented by big short investor Michael Burry reporting puts on Nvidia and Palantir (PLTR), SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) selling its Nvidia stake for $5.83B, and Peter Thiel selling his.

So, the results yesterday were a reminder of how much weight Nvidia holds on markets, plus it was a confirmation that while everything looks very messy, markets can rest assured that demand continues to outpace supply and Nvidia remains the winner of all the AI infrastructure CapEx spend heading into 2026.

We were worried about Nvidia’s Q4 outlook heading into Wednesday’s results. Our concern came from data points coming out of the AI server supply chain players: Super Micro Computer (SMCI), Wistron, Wiwynn, among others, reported higher Q/Q inventory levels, some Taiwanese ODMs reported softer October sales, and CoreWeave (CRWV) reported a disappointing FY25 outlook due to “temporary delays related to a third-party data center developer behind schedule.”

We put all these data points side by side, and given that CoreWeave is the first neocloud in line to get GB300, we expected this could point to potential production issues within the GB300 ramp. We didn’t see this thesis play out this quarter, but we are still watching for it. We think this could be an issue that shows up in Q4 earnings results if monthly sales reports continue to signal weakness, which would almost automatically signal an issue with the GB300 ramp. Any technical issues with GB300 aren’t out of the ordinary, given the higher density and complexity of the product line and the liquid-cooling requirements that come with it. Having said that, if we don’t see these technical issues resolved quickly, we believe this will negatively weigh on Nvidia’s Q4 results in February, big time. That’s the risk we’re watching.

We think Nvidia is aware of this potential issue and is addressing it. Digitimes reported on Monday morning that Nvidia is tightening its grip on the next-gen Vera Rubin cooling supply chain by consolidating assembly among three ODMs, likely to be Foxconn, Wistron, and Quanta. The reason we're seeing Nvidia race to consolidate its supply chain is that it's struggling to keep up with high yields for more complex product line-ups. According to vendors:

The company often pushes new products into mass production while designs are still changing. They also note that a new generation frequently appears before the previous platform achieves stable, full-run production yields.

This is probably showing up with the GB300 ramp now, and Nvidia wants to get ahead of the game in time for the Vera Rubin, which requires even more compute density and power consumption, which means liquid cooling. The current estimate is that liquid-cooling components per cabinet will double on the next-gen Vera Rubin. We don’t think the tech itself is flawed; instead, we think these are the growing pains of a tech transition, confronting extremely high expectations that leave little room for slip-ups.

Earnings call recap: Where’s China?

This quarter’s results and Q4 outlook, similar to last quarter, do not take into account any H20 sales to China. Collette Kress, Nvidia CFO, noted that:

While we were disappointed in the current state that prevents us from shipping more competitive data center compute products to China, we are committed to continued engagement with the U.S. and Chinese governments and will continue to advocate for America's ability to compete around the world.

We didn’t, however, get any questions on China, the +$50B market, during the Q&A section, and hence expectations for Nvidia’s China business continue to be tethered to geopolitical tensions. The fact that we didn’t see more China commentary suggests there is no new update, but, at the same time, the fact that we didn’t see the conversation about re-entering that market shut down is a positive in disguise. Our belief is that Nvidia’s next meaningful leg of outperformance will come from resuming its AI chip sales to China, both because it is the world’s second-largest AI market and because this would be the upside surprise the market isn’t expecting.

Last month, CEO Jensen Huang confirmed that “At the moment, we’re 100 per cent out of China.” Nvidia’s market share in China has dropped from 95% to 0%, with the greatest pushback to its resumption of sales coming from a combination of Beijing warning domestic players against purchasing Nvidia AI chips due to national security concerns and the Trump administration pushing back against allowing the watered-down Blackwell chip, B30A, to be sold to the nation.

The result of this is a rush among Chinese domestic players to fill Nvidia’s gap. Huawei is the face of this domestic push, unveiling its AI chip roadmap back in September. Alibaba (BABA) even came out with an AI chip, T-Head, that competes with H20. Cambricon Technologies, now labelled the “Nvidia of China,” reported that revenue grew 14x last quarter. The way Rachel Yeo put it:

[Cambricon’s growth is] one of the starkest signs yet of how China’s chipmakers are benefiting from a national drive to replace restricted Nvidia Corp. gear during a domestic AI development boom.

The pressure is on, and Beijing isn’t waiting for the U.S. to approve the sale of more advanced AI chips to China. So, why do we expect Nvidia to tap into this market again? The answer lies in the fundamentals: the performance gap between China’s AI chips and those of the rest of the world is widening and will continue to do so as AI moves to more advanced nodes next year, while China lags behind, stuck at 7nm due to the U.S. export controls.

We expect Beijing to cave as this gap widens, and the competitiveness of domestic solutions has a ticking clock on it. In a commentary with Bloomberg’s Ed Ludlow, Jensen emphasized that Nvidia’s share in China is zero repeatedly and so we think building up from 0% expectations for China will be the next big growth driver that could drive up Nvidia’s market cap to cross $5.5T potentially.

Bottom Line:

We continue to be bullish on Nvidia and expect the stock to be an outperformer in 2026. We believe Nvidia has the element of surprise working in its favor if it can get back into China. Nvidia is a survivor "cockroach," and so we see it as more resilient to the entire AI bubble and circular financing conversation. Because, at least for the short-term, tier 1 and hyperscaler players will continue to spend on speculative demand, and all roads of spend lead to more revenue for Nvidia.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bitcoin: How Many Chairs Should You Be Sitting On?
data.description:
Michael Saylor, CEO of Strategy Inc. (MSTR), recently likened his company being all-in on Bitcoin to sitting on the one correct chair. His chair metaphor implied Bitcoin is the best and only asset worth investing in today.

Today, with Bitcoin (BTC-USD) undergoing a correction of more than 25% since its recent all time high, I am revising my coverage for this cryptocurrency. In my past work I have discussed my bull and bear cases for BTC, why Bitcoin can be a global reserve asset (but not a global means of payment), and how a USD crisis is impacting it.

In today’s article I will focus on what exposure investors should consider to Bitcoin in their portfolios. Using Saylor’s metaphor, how many “chairs” should they be sitting on? This, in my view, is a question readers may ask themselves during the current market correction. Especially if they entered a position in Bitcoin recently, in hope of short-term returns.

Quick summary of my thoughts and thesis on Bitcoin

Personally, I see Bitcoin as the most obvious asymmetric bet in the market at the moment: the possibility of entering a new global reserve asset before it fully matures into one.

Bitcoin has all the characteristics it needs to become a superior global reserve asset: durability, divisibility, fungibility, portability, verifiability, and, most importantly in my view, scarcity.

The real question is whether the world's institutions will actually adopt it as a global reserve asset. To assess whether that is or isn’t a likely scenario, my valuation model for Bitcoin includes three scenarios and two cases:

  • A bullish case, with Bitcoin maturing to match Gold in market cap, at roughly $1,000,000 per coin.

  • A bearish case, with Bitcoin remaining an “online casino”, meaning an asset whose value is only derived from its volatility and short-term trading. In this scenario, I assign Bitcoin a valuation of $100,000 per coin, roughly where it has been trading for the last months.

The table below summarizes the outcome

Let’s talk about exposure to Bitcoin

The exact amount of exposure to Bitcoin in one’s portfolio depends on personal objectives, attitude towards risk, and overall thoughts about Bitcoin.

Anyone who is not convinced of my thesis or of Bitcoin’s technical characteristics should not be exposed to Bitcoin at all, for example. Conversely, risk-tolerant investors that are very bullish on Bitcoin may be fine with a significant exposure to it, up to a majority of their portfolios.

Another important thing to consider is that some investors may find themselves holding a significant portion of their portfolios in Bitcoin, even if their cost basis is not as significant. That is my case: having bought Bitcoin at an average price of ~$25,000 per coin, I do have an exposure to BTC that, at market prices, reached up to over 30% of my overall portfolio when Bitcoin hit its ATH.

Ultimately, allocating Bitcoin in one’s portfolio is an exercise that balances the potential for superior returns with the risk of volatility. With that in mind, I want to provide some food for thought on what exposure to Bitcoin readers should consider, using a specific use case, with the below assumptions:

  • A 5-year buy-and-hold period.

  • A comparison of Bitcoin to an investment in the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO).

  • Return figures roughly based on the last 5 years’ performance: a +400% return for BTC and a +90% return for VOO (rounded for simplicity).

  • A “stress test,” assuming that after the above returns, Bitcoin crashes 60% (entering a bear market) while VOO crashes 15%.

  • Two exposure scenarios: Investor A being exposed 100% to Bitcoin and Investor B being exposed 20% to Bitcoin and 80% to VOO.

The table below summarizes the return of such an exercise.

Scenario

Investor

Allocation

Component Outcomes

Total Portfolio Value

Portfolio Return

Bull Case

Investor A

$100,000 BTC

BTC → $500,000

$500,000

+400%

Investor B

$20,000 BTC $80,000 VOO

BTC → $100,000

VOO → $152,000

$252,000

+152%

Stress Test

Investor A

$100,000 BTC

BTC → $40,000

$40,000

−60%

Investor B

$20,000 BTC $80,000 VOO

BTC →

$8,000

VOO → $68,000

$76,000

−24%

The result, in my view, indicates how some Bitcoin exposure can significantly boost returns (based on historical data) while not having too much of an impact on volatility. Being fully exposed to Bitcoin, on the other hand, provides significant volatility that would see an investor losing most of their initial capital in case of a sharp correction.

Fine-tuning Bitcoin exposure

After having determined that a complete exposure to Bitcoin is not recommendable for most, a more interesting exercise concerns different levels of exposure. Should investors consider only a minor exposure to Bitcoin? Or should they consider up to half of their portfolio in Bitcoin?

To answer those questions, I created the following two additional scenarios, which feature two new types of investors:

  • Investor C, exposed to Bitcoin for 50%

  • Investor D, with a minor exposure to Bitcoin of 5%

The other assumptions remain exactly the same as the previous exercise. Below are the results.

Scenario

Investor

Allocation

Component Outcomes

Total Portfolio Value

Portfolio Return

Bull Case

Investor C

$50,000 BTC $50,000 VOO

BTC → $250,000 VOO → $95,000

$345,000

+245%

Investor D

$5,000 BTC $95,000 VOO

BTC → $25,000 VOO → $180,500

$205,500

+105.5%

Stress Test

Investor C

$50,000 BTC $50,000 VOO

BTC → $20,000 VOO → $42,500

$62,500

−37.5%

Investor D

$5,000 BTC $95,000 VOO

BTC → $2,000 VOO → $80,750

$82,750

−17.3%

What this new scenario indicates, in my view, is that a minimal exposure of 5% to Bitcoin serves little or no purpose today. It doesn’t provide significant upside (Investor D’s performance is significantly lower than Investor B), but it still drags down overall performance during a bear market. In other words, with Bitcoin having already matured to a multi-trillion-dollar asset, future reasonable returns are not enough to justify a minor exposure.

Bitcoin bulls should consider an exposure, in my view, of at least 15%-20% of their portfolio in order to see some benefits. That, of course, still comes with significant risks, which I will cover next.

Risks

I already mentioned at the beginning of this article that an investment in Bitcoin only makes sense for those who are bullish on it. As obvious as this sounds, this is something worth repeating, as I often get Bitcoin skeptics debating me in my BTC pieces, arguing that Bitcoin will never work. My entire work on Bitcoin, including this article, is based on the assumption that Bitcoin has everything it takes to mature into a global reserve asset, and that this outcome is more likely than not to happen.

In that regard, the main risk of any type of exposure to Bitcoin is that this cryptocurrency may never mature to a global reserve asset and rather trade close to the current range indefinitely.

Another risk concerns the fact that, today, BTC and the S&P 500 are not perfectly correlated. Pairing them reduces overall portfolio volatility while still allowing participation in Bitcoin’s upside. However, this is also based on historical data and may change in the future. If Bitcoin were to mature to a global reserve asset, it could also acquire the relatively low volatility of gold, the world’s global reserve asset today.

Conclusion: No, Bitcoin should not be your “only chair”

Even just five years ago, an argument could be made that an exposure to Bitcoin of 1%–5% of one’s portfolio could be beneficial. Today, Bitcoin bulls will find very little benefit from such a small exposure, which is certain to drag down their performance during bear markets but provides little upside overall.

This said, a complete exposure to Bitcoin is not preferable either, as it exposes investors to significant capital depletion. In that sense, I do not share Saylor’s idea that a total exposure to Bitcoin is not only desirable, but even “obvious.” I rather believe Bitcoin bulls should be exposed between 15% and 30% to Bitcoin in their portfolio, depending on their attitude towards risk and tolerance to volatility.

My “BUY” rating of Bitcoin for this article reflects my continued, bullish take on Bitcoin and my own exposure to BTC, which reflects my final recommendation.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Potential Winner With Value Trap Risks
data.description:
I previously covered Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) (OTCPK:NONOF) in August 2025, discussing how the compounding/regulatory headwinds had triggered its stock price corrections, worsened by the prior market rotation to AI-related stocks.

With NVO's tailwinds from the upcoming oral candidate launch negated by the sudden management change, I had believed that stock's correction presented an excellent dollar cost averaging opportunity - resulting in my Buy rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for NVO, thanks to the emergence of a double bottom indicator, the consistent bounce at the P/E support levels of 12x, and the recovery tailwinds arising from its oral GLP-1 candidate.

Otherwise, NVO also presents a potential value trap, attributed to the consistently lowered FY2025 guidance and numerous growth headwinds, with the stock only suitable for those with higher risk appetite.

NVO Presents A Mixed Investment Thesis

NVO 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy rating, NVO has notably underperformed the wider market and its GLP-1 therapy peers, partly attributed to the mixed FQ3'25 performance and the lowered FY2025 guidance for the fourth time.

This is on top of the growth headwinds arising from the drug price deals, the therapy's mixed efficacy rates, the GLP-1 price war, and the consistently lowered consensus forward estimates - all of which present its mixed near-term investment thesis.

1. Drug Price Deals

For one, I expect NVO to report a notably impacted top/bottom-line performance from FY2026 onwards, attributed to the drug-pricing deal across the "Medicare, Medicaid, as well as cash-paying customers, through a government-run website named TrumpRx."

This is a material development indeed, since NVO's Ozempic/Wegovy monthly prices are expected to be down notably from the prior range of "$1,000 and $1,350" to $245 for Medicare/Medicaid and $350 for TrumpRx.

Given that the US region comprises 62.6% of NVO's YTD Obesity care revenues, there is a great cause for concern indeed, as the management also hints at an impact of "a negative low single-digit impact on global sales growth for the full year of 2025" had the prices "been introduced first of January 2025."

While the Maximum Fair Price [MFP] for Medicare Part D is expected to be effective only from January 01, 2027 onwards, I am of the opinion that NVO's near-term performance is likely to underwhelm given the upcoming launch of TrumpRx in 2026.

2. Efficacy Rates

LLY's Expanding Market Share

This is especially since NVO's US incretin analogs market share has moderated to 41.7% in Q3'25 (-0.8 points QoQ/YoY data unavailable), as Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) continues to gain share and the latter's "Zepbound exited Q3 with 71% share of new prescriptions."

It is apparent from these developments that NVO's strategic partnership with CVS Health (CVS) since July 01, 2025 has not worked out as intended in driving renewed US growth prospects.

Part of the headwinds may attributed to NVO Wegovy's lower weight loss rate at -13.7% of the patients' body weight by week 72, compared to LLY Mounjaro's accelerated weight loss rate at -20% and Zepbound's at -20.2%.

Competition is heating up as well, with Viking Therapeutics' (VKTX) VK2735 delivering a weight loss rate of -14.7% by week 13 and Pfizer's (PFE) acquired pipeline from Metsera, MET-097i, also delivering an excellent average weight loss rate of -14.1% by week 28 and a maximum of 26.5% in select patients.

While both pipelines are still in the Clinical Phase 3 and 2, respectively, NVO will undeniably meet intense competition once these candidates are approved by the US FDA and launched commercially sometime near the end of the decade.

These reasons may be why NVO has previously started a bidding war for Metsera by late October 2025, albeit ultimately losing out to PFE attributed to the US Federal Trade Commission's opposition.

3. Price War

NVO's prospects have been further worsened by the growing compounding market in the US:

Novo Nordisk market research shows that compounding has continued to increase. Multiple entities continue to market and sell compounded GLP-1s and it is now estimated to be well above 1 million patients in the U.S. that are currently on compounded GLP-1. (Seeking Alpha)

At a time when NVO's GLP-1 prices are impacted, compounded GLP-1 offerings are likely to remain headwinds as well, as Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) "strategically reduced prices across compounded GLP-1 treatment plans by as much as 20%," with injectables now costing $199 monthly and oral therapies $69 monthly.

This has also led to NVO lowering the prices of the two lowest doses of Wegovy and Ozempic for an introductory period through March 2026, to match those currently offered by LLY - with it marking a potentially prolonged and painful price war.

With compounded GLP-1 offerings likely to remain popular attributed to their improved long-term affordability, I am of the opinion the NVO's prospects are likely to remain mixed moving forward.

This is especially since the cheaper prices (arising from Medicare/Medicaid/TrumpRx) are likely to set a new normal in GLP-1 therapy prices, with the price erosion already triggering a notably smaller anti-obesity global market size of $95B by 2030, down from the prior estimates of $130B.

Combined with the increased availability of GLP-1 therapies from numerous market players over the next few years, I am of the opinion that these offerings may eventually be less of a novelty and more of a commodity, with it likely eroding NVO's first market mover advantage.

4. NVO's Cheap Valuations May Be A Value Trap

The Consensus Forward Estimates

As a result, I can understand why the consensus have also lowered their forward estimates, with NVO expected to report impacted top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +5.3%/+3% through FY2027.

This is a direct contrast to LLY's consistently raised estimates at +24.7%/+44% over the same time period, with it exemplifying the market's extreme pessimism surrounding NVO's future prospects, against the latter's 5Y historical growth profile at +18.9%/+22.5%, respectively.

NVO Valuations

As a result of the underwhelming GLP-1 monetization trends and the consistently downgraded FY2025 guidance, it is unsurprising that NVO has been consistently downgraded to FWD EV/Sales valuations of 4.71x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 13.43x by the time of writing.

This is down notably from the 5Y mean of 10.38x/30.34x, the 10Y mean of 8.27x/24.41x, and the sector median of 3.82x/19.07x, respectively.

Based on the current Enterprise Value of $225.77B and the FY2030 revenue estimates of $66.09B, NVO appears rather discounted at my estimated FY2030 EV/Sales valuations of 3.41x as well, compared to its GLP-1 peers, including:

  • GLP-1 leader - LLY at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 14.98x/my estimated FY2030 EV/Sales valuations of 8.37x (using the same methodology),
  • GLP-1 candidate through Metsera acquisition, PFE at 3.03x/3.16x,
  • pre-revenue GLP-1 player - VKTX at NA/2.57x, respectively.

NVO Technical Indicators

On the other hand, NVO's technical indicators remain mixed at current levels, as the stock charts lower lows and lower highs despite losing -66.4% of its value since the June 2024 heights.

While I have observed the potential emergence of NVO's double bottom at the $45s by the time of writing, with it signaling a possible change in its future stock price momentum from the prior meltdown, it remains to be seen when sentiments may recover.

Author's Historical Rating

While I have repeatedly highlighted NVO's robust capital appreciation opportunity upon a reversal in its performance metrics and an upward re-rating in its valuations, it is also apparent that my prior Buy ratings have not worked out as expected.

Pending the emergence of its bullish support and/or further growth catalysts, readers may want to note the risks of NVO potentially being a value trap at current levels, with a breach of the false double bottom a possible outcome.

NVO's Reversal Hinges On Outsized Oral Therapy Outperformance

NVO 5Y Stock Price

With NVO effectively losing all of its 4Y gains, it remains to be seen if the double bottom may be sufficient to convince the bulls to finally defend a trading floor, attributed to its mixed prospects from the numerous headwinds discussed above.

I am of the opinion that its near-term recovery prospects hinge on NVO's ability to successfully market its oral obesity/diabetes candidate in 2026, namely the once-daily Wegovy upon the upcoming US FDA approval sometime by the end of 2025.

My optimism is attributed to NVO's oral Wegovy delivering an improved weight loss rate at -16.6% by week 64, compared to LLY's orforglipron at -10.5% by week 52, with it presenting a potential catalyst to the former's growth prospects, since most patients typically prefer oral delivery at 62% compared to injection at 16%.

This is especially since NVO has already forged numerous partnerships/channels to rapidly launch the oral GLP-1 therapy upon approval while "already started manufacturing the oral GLP-1 ahead of the FDA approval decision," with it implying a rapid commercialization cadence right from the start.

As a result of its first to market advantage and the improved weight loss results, I believe that NVO may very well capture a robust oral therapy market share moving forward, against Goldman Sachs Research's expectation of " daily oral pills will capture some 25% share of the anti-obesity medication market by the end of the decade."

These imply a potentially drastic reversal in NVO's prior underperformance, the consequent upgrading in its current P/E valuations of 13.43x nearer to the 5Y P/E mean of 30.34x (cheap compared to LLY at 43.39x), and the projected doubling in its stock prices beyond current levels.

While it may be hard to time the bottom, I am of the opinion that the stock offers a relatively compelling risk reward profile at current levels, aided by the consistent bounce at the P/E floor of 12x during the worst of the August/November 2025 sell-off.

This is on top of the overly priced in growth headwinds, the oral candidate tailwinds, the rich cash flow generation, and the growing book value per share at $38.23 (+41.2% YoY) - with a floor likely to emerge sooner than later.

I am reiterating my Buy rating for the NVO stock here, while similarly looking to initiate a small position near to the 52 weeks bottom of ~$45.

It goes without saying that NVO may be a value trap at current levels, as discussed above, with my reiterated Buy rating only suitable for those with a higher risk appetite and a longer investing trajectory.
